perturbation.original_text: All products and results of RECIPIENT’S services rendered hereunder (the "Work") are works made for hire. RECIPIENT acknowledge s and agree that the Work (and all rights therein, including, without limitation, copyrights) belongs to and shall be the sole and exclusive property of OWNER.
perturbation.changed_text: All products and results of RECIPIENT’S services rendered hereunder (the "Work") are presumed to be works made for hire, unless otherwise agreed in writing. RECIPIENT acknowledges and agrees that the Work (and all rights therein, including, without limitation, copyrights) belongs to OWNER, except where RECIPIENT can demonstrate prior ownership.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity regarding ownership of the Work. The original text clearly states that all work is made for hire and belongs exclusively to the OWNER. The modified text allows for exceptions based on written agreements or prior ownership by the RECIPIENT. This contradicts the original assignment described in point 2 of Section III. A. Title and Copyright Assignment, which states: 'RECIPIENT also hereby assigns and transfers to OWNER, its successors and assigns, the entire right, title, and interest in and to all copyrights in the Work'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Not withstanding the foregoing, RECIPIENT also hereby assigns and transfers to OWNER, its successors and assigns, the entire right, title, and interest in and to all copyrights in the Work; all registrations and copyright applications relating thereto and all renewals and extensions thereof; all works ba sed upon, derived from, or incorporating the Work; all income, royalties, damages, claims an d payments now or hereafter due or payable with respect thereto; all causes of action, either in law or in equity for past, present, or future infringement based on the copyrights; and all rights corresponding to the foregoing throughout the world.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that all work belongs exclusively to the OWNER. The changed text introduces exceptions allowing the RECIPIENT to claim prior ownership or introduce written agreements. This directly contradicts the contradicted text (point 2), which states that the RECIPIENT assigns and transfers the entire right, title, and interest in all copyrights of the Work to the OWNER. The change creates ambiguity and uncertainty regarding copyright ownership where the original contract clearly assigns it all to the OWNER. This introduces a strong in-text contradiction as per the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT shall not institute any action or suit at law or in equity against OWNER, nor institute, prosecute or in any way aid in the institution or prosecution of any claim, demand, action, or cause of action arising out of the INFORMATION or any INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY thereof, including but not limited to, claim, demand, action, or cause of action for in validating any INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY of OWNER.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT shall not institute any action or suit at law or in equity against OWNER related to the INFORMATION disclosed, provided that the OWNER is acting in good faith. This restriction does not apply if OWNER is engaging in willful misconduct or gross negligence related to the INFORMATION or any INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY thereof, including but not limited to, claim, demand, action, or cause of action for in validating any INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY of OWNER.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a 'good faith' exception to the covenant not to sue. The original covenant was absolute, prohibiting any legal action by the RECIPIENT against the OWNER. The modified version allows the RECIPIENT to sue if the OWNER acts in bad faith or with gross negligence. This creates an internal contradiction, since the section V. DAMAGES AND SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE states that if RECIPIENT breaches a ny of the promises contained in this Agreement that OWNER would suffer irreparable harm and OWNER w ould be without adequate remedy at law and that OWNER may obtain injunctive relief, including specific performance of the Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: V. DAMAGES AND SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE RECIPIENT agrees that should RECIPIENT breach a ny of the promises contained in this Agreement that OWNER would suffer irreparable harm and OWNER w ould be without adequate remedy at law and that OWNER may obtain injunctive relief, including specific performance of the Agreement, as well as monetary award for damages suffered by OWNER for RECIPIENT’S breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text prevented the RECIPIENT from suing the OWNER under any circumstances. The changed text now allows the RECIPIENT to sue the OWNER under specific circumstances (willful misconduct or gross negligence). This directly contradicts section V, which states that OWNER would suffer irreparable harm if RECIPIENT breaches any promise and that OWNER may obtain injunctive relief. The changed text now creates a scenario where RECIPIENT could potentially sue, which would constitute a breach, creating a strong internal contradiction regarding OWNER's remedy and RECIPIENT's ability to take legal action.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California in the Central District of California. Any dispute involving the terms or conditions of this Agreement shall be brought in the Central District of California or a California State court of competent subject matter jurisdiction therein. Each of the parties he reby submits to the personal jurisdiction of said court.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to its conflict of laws principles. Any dispute involving the terms or conditions of this Agreement may be brought in the Central District of California or a California State court of competent subject matter jurisdiction therein, but the parties agree to first attempt mediation in good faith. Each of the parties he reby submits to the personal jurisdiction of said court.
perturbation.explanation: The introduction of a mandatory mediation clause prior to litigation creates a contradiction. The original agreement stipulated that any dispute 'shall' be brought in a specific court. The changed text introduces a condition precedent (mediation) before legal action can be pursued, this can create contradiction in Section V. DAMAGES AND SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE, since there is no longer a prompt legal process as stipulated in that Section to solve the problem by OWNER.
perturbation.contradicted_text: V. DAMAGES AND SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE RECIPIENT agrees that should RECIPIENT breach a ny of the promises contained in this Agreement that OWNER would suffer irreparable harm and OWNER w ould be without adequate remedy at law and that OWNER may obtain injunctive relief, including specific performance of the Agreement, as well as monetary award for damages suffered by OWNER for RECIPIENT’S breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulated that disputes 'shall' be brought in a specific court. The changed text introduces mandatory mediation before litigation, creating a condition precedent that did not exist before. This contradicts Section V, which allows the OWNER to obtain injunctive relief, including specific performance, without any mention of mandatory mediation. This contradiction weakens the OWNER's ability to promptly seek legal recourse as initially stipulated in Section V, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: To immediately return to OWNER all of the items in the possession of RECIPIENT which relate to or which disclose in whole or in part any confidential information of OWNER; and
perturbation.changed_text: To immediately return to OWNER all of the items in the possession of RECIPIENT which relate to or which disclose in whole or in part any confidential information of OWNER, provided that RECIPIENT may retain one copy of such items for archival purposes; and
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the RECIPIENT to retain a copy of confidential information, directly contradicting the obligation to return 'all' items. This contradicts the central premise of the agreement, which is to protect the OWNER's confidential information. Keeping a copy introduce in-text contradiction with section I.C., which states 'RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement.'. Now, the 'RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNER' is not valid anymore, since the RECIPIENT can hold the information to be used afterwards.
perturbation.contradicted_text: C. Notwithstanding paragraph B, RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement. With respect to any INFORMATION known by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNER that RECIPIENT believes to constitute the INFORMATION, or any portion thereof, RECIPIENT shall disclose to OWNER an adequate written description of the INFORMATION within fourteen (14) days of the disclosure by OWNER.
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the RECIPIENT to retain a copy of confidential information, while the contradicted text (C) states RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement. The change introduces a new way for RECIPIENT to have the OWNER's information, thereby contradicting section C, which only permits use if the information was already known to the RECIPIENT. As such, there is a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: OWNER agrees to disclose INFORMATION to RE CIPIENT to facilitate possible future business dealings between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: OWNER may, at its sole discretion, disclose INFORMATION to RE CIPIENT to facilitate possible future business dealings between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that OWNER 'agrees to disclose' which implies an obligation. The changed text states that OWNER 'may...disclose' at its 'sole discretion', which removes the obligation and makes it optional. This creates ambiguity because it changes the fundamental understanding of the OWNER's commitment to sharing information, which implies in the whereas clause that 'OWNER desires to disclose the INFORM ATION on a confidential basis to RECIPIENT solely for the purposes of evaluating the INFORMATI ON for possible future business arrangements'. If OWNER can disclose at it's sole discretion, it can violate the intentions described in the whereas.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, OWNER has developed through substantia l effort, research, time, and expense certain inventions, design concepts, methodologies, technical know-how, copyrightable material and trade secrets directed and related to______________________________________________________ (“INFORMATION”); WHEREAS, OWNER desires to disclose the INFORM ATION on a confidential basis to RECIPIENT solely for the purposes of evaluating the INFORMATI ON for possible future business arrangements; and WHEREAS, OWNER wishes to maintain the confidentiality of the INFORMATION and the protection of OWNER'S intellectual property rights.
perturbation.justification: The original text states 'agrees to disclose,' indicating an obligation for the OWNER to disclose INFORMATION. The changed text modifies this to 'may...disclose' at its 'sole discretion,' effectively removing the mandatory obligation and making disclosure optional. This contradicts the 'WHEREAS' clause, which states 'OWNER desires to disclose the INFORM ATION on a confidential basis to RECIPIENT,' creating an inconsistency because if the OWNER now has sole discretion, they are no longer committed to disclosing as was the original intent, thereby creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined by the Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction definition, as the term disclosure is defined inconsistently within the document.
